title: Euodia oblongifolia
taxonomy: Euodia oblongifolia is classified within the family Rutaceae, commonly known as the citrus or rue family. This classification places it among other flowering plants that produce fragrant oils and have similar leaf and flower characteristics. Euodia oblongifolia is also classified within the genus Euodia, which contains several species of flowering plants native to Asia. The genus Euodia is part of the tribe Rutoideae, which includes several genera of flowering plants that share similar characteristics. By classifying Euodia oblongifolia within the Rutaceae family, scientists can better understand its evolutionary relationships and unique characteristics.

intro: Euodia oblongifolia, also known as Huang Qi Cao, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rutaceae, commonly found in East Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, offering potential health benefits such as reducing inflammation, improving immune function, and protecting against various diseases. The bioactive constituents of Euodia oblongifolia include limonoids, alkaloids, and flavonoids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, Euodia oblongifolia is often processed into herbal preparations such as decoctions, teas, and infusions, as well as being combined with other herbs to create formulas for treating various health conditions.
